text: Welcome to the series of videos of Versius thoracic robotic surgery with Sven Seifert and his fabulous team in Chemnitz, Germany. In this video we're going to show you a nice upper lobe wedge resection.
The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy on the right side of the chest using the Versius robotic system, focusing on the precise removal of lymphatic tissue.
The surgeon inserts the first and second anterior ports, followed by a posterior port, all measuring five millimeters in diameter. A ten-millimeter endoscope is used for visualization.

A bipolar grasper is introduced at the top middle of the surgical field, positioned by the assistant for robotic control. The Maryland dissector is brought in on the right side, ready for use in the procedure.
An external view shows the port placement on the patient's thoracic region, with instrument base units marked by colors instead of numbers.

The surgeon positions two 5mm ports on the right side and one on the left side of the chest. The surgeon selects the purple instrument for the left hand and the Maryland grasper for the right hand, preparing to proceed with the surgical tasks.
The surgeon uses a 5mm Maryland instrument in the right hand and a bipolar grasper in the left hand, demonstrating a combination of tools for the esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the right upper lobe of the lung, visually inspecting the apical nodule suspected to be cancerous. The plan involves performing a wedge resection for diagnostic purposes, with a lymphadenectomy already completed if the nodule is confirmed as cancer.
The surgeon uses the grasper and Maryland instrument to locate the tumor within the chest cavity, with swabs visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses CO2 insufflation at a pressure of six to eight millimeters of mercury to achieve lung collapse, providing a clear view of the surgical field for manipulation.

The camera is positioned at the eighth intercostal space, providing a view of the chest cavity. The surgeon locates the nodule at the top of the chest and assesses the optimal approach for further action.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the nodule located in the right upper lobe, assessing the optimal site for wedge resection. An assistant port, measuring twelve millimeters, is utilized for instrument access.
The assistant uses a Cavidian purple tri-stapler to perform a stapling action, as the Versius robotic system does not have its own stapler.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to stabilize the tissue sample and applies a purple tri-stapler to create a long line of staples under the wedge resection. A spare cyan instrument unit is visible on the right side of the surgical field.
The surgical team completes the wedge resection and leaves the resected tissue in the chest to maintain the CO2 seal, while the assistant retracts the line.
The surgeon retracts the hilum anteriorly to expose the posterior hilum and the subcranial station, while an assistant holds a swab in place.

The surgeon identifies the azygos vein at the top of the surgical field, marking the upper boundary of the dissection. An incision is made down the back of the lung, with the lung tissue forming the right-sided margin and the vagus nerve forming the left-sided margin. The dissection proceeds over the bronchus intermedius and just below it.

The surgeon performs a lymph node dissection, aiming to visualize both bronchi, including the left-sided bronchi. The vagus nerve is visible on the left side, while Sven maintains position just to the left of the lung tissue.
The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to manage small branches of the vagus nerve, with Versius robotic system offering hooks and spatulas for monopolar options.
The surgeon uses a bipolar Maryland instrument to dissect around the bronchus intermedius, pulling it back with a grasper for better visualization and minimizing instrument changes.

The surgeon uses a grasper to hold the station seven lymph node while the assistant positions instruments. The dissection proceeds close to the bronchus intermedius, extending up to the carina to remove the subcarinal station as a single unit.

The Versius robot has a very innovative hand control system. Because you use your first finger to open and close the instruments, you've got your thumbs completely free for two joysticks that control the camera and also the menu boards for several other features. So you don't need any foot plates. The advantage of this is that you can stand up or sit down.

The surgeon uses a robotic system to remove the station seven lymph node from the subcarinal station, placing it into the thumb of a glove for safe extraction through the assistant port. The procedure is viewed on a 3D screen with goggles.
The surgeon uses swabs to maintain a dry surgical field, performing a final check to ensure all lymph nodes, including those tucked under the bronchus intermedius, have been meticulously removed.

The surgeon navigates distally along the bronchus intermedius, approaching the bifurcation between the lower and middle lobes. The dissection targets lymph nodes potentially classified as station R-ten, but due to difficulty in distinguishing them from station seven, the surgeon opts to remove them as part of station seven.
The surgeon has successfully emptied the subcarinal station, leaving the lymph node in place. The bipolar grasper is positioned near the left main bronchus, with plans to improve the view later.
The surgeon identifies the bronchial artery originating from the descending aorta, located under the carina and near its apex, ensuring careful management of the blood supply during the procedure.

The surgeon uses diathermy to remove the final part of the subcarinal lymph node station, employing the thumb of a glove technique to safely extract the lymph nodes without loss through the assistant port.

The surgeon uses a robotic system with five-millimeter instruments to pull the hilum posteriorly while the assistant pushes the lung back. The team then visually inspects the anterior station ten lymph nodes before proceeding to examine the station two or four lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies the azygos vein and superior vena cava (SVC), beginning the dissection of station 2R/4 lymph nodes just above the azygos. The pleura is stripped along the SVC to expose the area, with the posterior dissection involving the vagus nerve. Lymph nodes are gathered into the middle of the area for removal, with the azygos vein visible beneath the grasper.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to lift out lymph nodes from the station, demonstrating hemostatic control. The bipolar energy effectively clamps down on smaller branches, preventing bleeding from veins near the SVC. A lymph node from the station is visible being extracted.
The surgeon identifies and removes a IV-R lymph node located just above the azygos vein, using a glove thumb technique for extraction.

The surgeon dissects anterior station XR lymph nodes below the ozygous vein, providing a clear view of the trachea and right main bronchus both under and over the ozygous. A small lymph node is visible tucked under the ozygous vein.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the 4R lymph nodes, located completely above the inferior border of the ozygous vein. The ozygous vein and trachea are visible, with the trachea stripped underneath the ozygous. The lymph nodes are safely contained within a retrieval bag, minimizing the risk of seeding or accidental loss through the port.

The surgeon removes swabs from the surgical field, revealing a clear view of the subcarinal station. The carina is visible, with both the left and right main bronchi appearing completely empty of lymph nodes. The back of the left atrium is also visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to perform a complete lymphadenectomy, removing lymph nodes from the surgical field. The wedge is taken out at the end, ensuring all removed tissue is contained in a bag. The procedure is executed using the Versius robotic system.

well done do tell us what you think of this very novel thoracic robotic system it's small it's nimble it's lots of space for the assistant and we're going to be posting more videos in this stream so look out for them and tell us what you think i hope you've enjoyed this video of a right-sided wedge and station seven station two or four are lymphadenectomy with a little bit of tenon as a bonus Thanks for watching and do watch again.
